docs.title: Software Engineering Lernpfad
docs.text: Ein umfassender Lernpfad mit 182 Themen und detaillierten Diagrammen zu Software Engineering.

docs.title: Statistik
docs.text: Metrik Wert Themen 182 Kategorien 10

docs.title: Kategorien
docs.text:
• :material-code: Programming — 13 Themen
  • :material-computer: Operating Systems — 19 Themen
  • Databases — 15 Themen
  • Networking — 23 Themen
  • Security — 9 Themen
  • :material-architecture: System Design — 10 Themen
  • Cloud & DevOps — 14 Themen
  • :material-smart_toy: AI — 3 Themen
  • Career — 6 Themen
  • Miscellaneous — 70 Themen

Basierend auf LinkedIn Tech Posts Archiv

docs.title: ChatGPT System Architecture and Workflow
docs.text:
How does ChatGPT-like System Work?

The two main phases of a ChatGPT-like system. The top section details the 'Training' phase, broken down into Stage 1 (Pre-training on internet data) and Stage 2 (Fine-tuning using demonstration data, a reward model, and PPO reinforcement learning). The bottom section details the 'Answer a prompt' phase, showing the flow from a user prompt through content moderation, safety checks, response generation by the ChatGPT model, and final output filtering.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Data Structures and Real-World Applications
docs.text:
10 Data Structures Used in Daily Life

A table illustrating 10 fundamental data structures (List, Array, Stack, Queue, Heap, Tree, Suffix Tree, Graph, R-tree, Hash Table) with visual diagrams and their corresponding real-world use cases, such as Twitter feeds for Lists and Undo/Redo for Stacks.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: GenAI Ecosystem and Workflow
docs.text:
Generative AI

The Generative AI landscape, covering key concepts from basic definitions and foundation models to transformers, training, development stacks, applications, and the AI engineer job role.

📊 Vergrößern

3 Themen verfügbar

docs.title: Full Stack Development Ecosystem
docs.text:
Full Stack Developer Roadmap

The comprehensive roadmap for a Full Stack Developer. It features a central developer figure connected to eight key domains: Web Frontend (Basics, Frameworks, Styles), Backend (Programming, Middleware, Communication), Mobile (Native & Cross Platform), Cloud (Providers, Containers), Database (RDBMS, NoSQL), UI/UX (Design Tools), Infrastructure (Nginx, Cloudflare), and CI/CD DevOps (Jenkins, Git, IaC).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Influential Computer Science and Engineering Papers
docs.text:
25 Papers That Completely Transformed the Computer World

Listing 25 highly impactful computer science papers and systems, such as Dynamo, Google File System, Attention Is All You Need, and Bitcoin, along with brief descriptions of their contributions to the field.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Interview Preparation Categories and Tools
docs.text:
Tech Interviews

Titled 'My Recommended Resources For Tech Interviews' that categorizes study materials and platforms into six main areas: Coding (Leetcode, Neetcode, Cracking the Coding Interview), System Design (Books, Grokking, Designing Data-Intensive Applications), Behavioral Interview (Tech Interview Handbook, A Life Engineered, STAR Method), OOD Interview (Interviewready, Educative, Head First Design Patterns), Mock Interview (Interviewing.io, Pramp, Meetapro), and Apply for Jobs (LinkedIn, Monster, Indeed).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Recommended reading list for software developers
docs.text:
10 Soft Skill Books Every Developer Should Read

Displaying 10 book recommendations for developers, categorized into four quadrants: Communication Skills, Productivity & Personal Development, Leadership & Team Dynamics, and Design & Craftsmanship.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Software Engineering Competency Map
docs.text:
Junior to Senior Developer Roadmap

The step-by-step technical skills, tools, and concepts required for a developer to advance from a junior to a senior level. It covers 11 key areas including collaboration tools, programming languages, API development, web servers, cloud infrastructure, authentication, testing, databases, CI/CD, data structures & algorithms, system design, design patterns, and AI tools.

📊 Vergrößern

5 Themen verfügbar

docs.title: AWS Service Portfolio and Classification
docs.text:
AWS Cloud Services

A circular infographic titled 'AWS Cloud Services Cheat Sheet' by ByteByteGo. It organizes numerous Amazon Web Services (AWS) products into functional categories arranged in a wheel around a central AWS logo. Categories include Analytics, Application Integration, Financial Management, Compute, Database, Developer Tools, Network & Content Delivery, Storage, and Identity & Compliance/Security.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Cloud Messaging Patterns
docs.text:
Top 6 Cloud Messaging Patterns

Six common cloud messaging patterns: Async Request-Reply, Publisher-Subscriber, Claim Check, Priority Queue, Saga, and Competing Consumers. Each pattern includes a visual diagram of the workflow and a list of specific use cases.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Docker Client, Host, and Registry Interaction
docs.text:
How does Docker Work?

The Docker workflow, showing how the Docker Client sends commands (build, pull, run) to the Daemon on the Docker Host, which manages Images and Containers, while interacting with the Docker Registry.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Docker Ecosystem and Architecture
docs.text:
8 Must-Know Docker Concepts

Eight fundamental Docker concepts. It covers the Dockerfile instructions, the composition of a Docker Image (code, dependencies, libraries), the creation of Containers from images, the role of a Docker Registry, the use of Volumes for file sharing, Docker Compose for multi-container management, Docker Networks for container communication, and the Docker CLI client-daemon interaction. A central panel humorously explains Docker's origin story regarding the 'works on my machine' problem.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Equivalent Cloud Services Mapping
docs.text:
Cloud Comparison Cheat Sheet

A comprehensive comparison chart displaying equivalent cloud services across four major providers: AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Oracle Cloud. The chart categorizes services into areas such as compute, storage, networking, databases, analytics, machine learning, and security management.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Kubernetes Cluster Components
docs.text:
What is k8s?

The high-level architecture of a Kubernetes (k8s) cluster. It depicts the user interface layer (Admin UI, CLI), the Control Plane (containing the API Server, Scheduler, etcd, and Control Manager), and the Worker Nodes (running Docker, kubelet, kube-proxy, and Pods with containers).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Kubernetes Design Patterns Classification
docs.text:
Top 10 k8s Design Patterns

Ten essential design patterns for Kubernetes (k8s). The patterns are grouped into four main categories: Structural (Init Container, Sidecar), Behavioural (Batch Job, Stateful Service, Stateful Discovery), Foundational (Health Probe, Automated Placement, Predictable Demands), and Higher-Level (Operator, Controller). Each section includes diagrams illustrating the architecture and data flow of the specific patterns.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Kubernetes Tools Stack Wheel
docs.text:
Kubernetes Tools Stack

A circular diagram (wheel) centered around the Kubernetes logo, categorizing various tools and technologies into six main sectors: Monitoring and Observability, Infrastructure/Cloud Orchestration, Networking, Cluster Management, Security, and Container Runtime. Each sector contains logos and names of specific tools like Prometheus, Terraform, Docker, Helm, and Istio.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Load Balancer Selection and Mapping
docs.text:
Cloud Load Balancer

A comprehensive cheat sheet featuring a decision tree flowchart for selecting the appropriate cloud load balancer based on requirements (Internet Facing, Global/Regional, SSL Offload, Hosting Type) and a side-by-side comparison table of equivalent services for AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Microsoft Azure Service Portfolio
docs.text:
Azure Cloud Services

A circular cheat sheet diagram illustrating the ecosystem of Microsoft Azure cloud services. The services are grouped into ten color-coded sectors: Networking, Compute, Containers, Storage, Database, Analytics, Blockchain, IoT, Security, and Multimedia & ML. Each sector lists specific services with their corresponding icons, such as Virtual Machines, Blob Storage, SQL DB, and Cognitive Services.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Monitoring Elements Comparison
docs.text:
Monitoring

A comprehensive cheat sheet table comparing monitoring tools and services across AWS, Google Cloud, Azure, and Open Source/3rd Party solutions. The comparison is categorized by monitoring elements including Data Collection, Data Storage, Data Analysis, Alerting, Visualization, Reporting and Compliance, Automation, Integration, and Feedback Loop.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Multi-Cloud Service Mapping
docs.text:
Cloud Comparison

A comprehensive cheat sheet comparing cloud services across four major providers: AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Oracle Cloud. The chart aligns equivalent services side-by-side, covering categories such as compute (VMs, Kubernetes), storage (object, block, file), networking (VPC, DNS, Load Balancing), databases (SQL, NoSQL), analytics, machine learning, and management/security tools.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Nginx Architecture, High-Performance Web Server, Reverse Proxy, Content Cache, SSL Termination
docs.text:
Why is Nginx so popular?

Titled 'Why is Nginx so popular?' centered around a 'Nginx Cheat Sheet' wheel. It details five key aspects of Nginx: its Master-Worker architecture, its function as a High-Performance Web Server handling concurrent connections, its role as a Reverse Proxy and Load Balancer distributing traffic, its Content Cache mechanism for fast rendering, and its SSL Termination capabilities for offloading decryption.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: RTO, RPO, and Disaster Recovery Architectures
docs.text:
Cloud Disaster Recovery Strategies

Cloud Disaster Recovery. The top section defines Recovery Point Objective (RPO) and Recovery Time Objective (RTO) on a timeline relative to a disaster. The main body details four specific strategies: 1. Backup and Restore, 2. Pilot Light, 3. Warm Standby, and 4. Multi Site, illustrating the architecture, data sync, and scaling for each approach.

📊 Vergrößern

14 Themen verfügbar

docs.title: DB Scaling Strategies
docs.text:
Database Scaling

Titled 'Database Scaling Cheatsheet' that illustrates seven core strategies for scaling databases: Indexing, Materialized Views, Denormalization, Vertical Scaling, Database Caching, Replication, and Sharding. Each strategy is accompanied by a diagram and a brief explanation of its function.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Data Sharding Strategies
docs.text:
Top 4 Data Sharding Algorithms

Four common algorithms for data sharding: Range-Based (partitioning by value ranges), Hash-Based (using a hash function modulo), Consistent Hashing (using a ring structure to minimize redistribution), and Virtual Bucket Hashing (mapping logical to physical buckets). Each section includes a visual diagram of the mechanism and a warning about potential downsides like uneven distribution or overhead.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Database Concurrency Control
docs.text:
9 Types of Database Locks

Nine distinct types of database locks: Shared (S), Exclusive (X), Update (U), Schema, Bulk Update (BU), Key Range, Row-Level, Page-Level, and Table-Level locks. Each section defines the lock's purpose and provides a diagram showing how multiple clients interact with a database table (Order ID, User ID, Price) under that specific lock type.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Database Data Structures and Indexing
docs.text:
8 Data Structures That Power Your Databases

A comprehensive table illustrating 8 key data structures used in databases: Skiplist, Hash index, SSTable, LSM tree, B-tree, Inverted index, Suffix tree, and R-tree. The table includes visual diagrams for each, their primary use cases (In-memory, Disk-based, Search), and notes on real-world applications like Redis and Lucene.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Database Sharding
docs.text:
A Crash Course on Database Sharding

The core concepts of database sharding. It covers the definition and benefits (scalability, performance, availability), different types of sharding (range-based, directory-based, key-based), criteria for selecting shard keys (cardinality, frequency, monotonic change), and strategies for routing requests to shards.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Full-Text Search, Real-Time Analytics, Machine Learning, Geo-Data Applications, Log Analysis, Security Information and Event Management
docs.text:
ElasticSearch

Six primary use cases for ElasticSearch, featuring architectural diagrams for Full-Text Search (with inverted indices), Real-Time Analytics (using Flink), Machine Learning (anomaly detection), Geo-Data Applications (k-d tree indexing), Log Analysis (ELK stack pipeline), and Security Information and Event Management (SIEM).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: History of Redis Features and Architecture
docs.text:
Redis Architecture

The evolution of Redis architecture from 2010 to 2020. It details key milestones including the transition from standalone to persistent storage (AOF/RDB), replication, high availability with Sentinel, sharding with Redis Cluster, the introduction of Streams, and the implementation of multi-threaded I/O.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Relational Database Design Overview
docs.text:
Cheatsheet for Relational Database Design

The basics of relational databases. It covers SQL definitions and CRUD operations, types of keys (Primary, Natural, Foreign) with code examples, visual representations of Join Types (Inner, Left, Right), Relationship Types (One-to-One, One-to-Many, Many-to-Many), and fundamental structural concepts like Tables, Rows, Columns, Indexes, and Views.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Relational, Time-series, and NoSQL Database Comparison
docs.text:
Database Systems

A flowchart diagram categorizing databases into three main types: Relational/SQL, Time-series, and NoSQL. For each type, it lists popular examples (such as MySQL, InfluxDB, and MongoDB) and details their specific characteristics, including features like ACID properties, high-write performance, and various data models like document-based or key-value stores.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: SQL Command Categories and Components
docs.text:
How to Learn SQL

A comprehensive mind map illustrating the structure of SQL, breaking it down into major categories like DDL, DQL, DML, DCL, and TCL, along with associated operators, functions, and data types.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: SQL Query Execution Order
docs.text:
SQL

The logical order in which SQL clauses are processed. It visualizes the transformation of data tables (grids) step-by-step through clauses like FROM, JOIN, ON, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, and LIMIT, contrasting the execution order with the written query order.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Top 6 Database Models
docs.text:
Database Architecture

Six common database models: Flat Model (represented by Excel), Hierarchical Model (tree structure with XML/JSON), Relational Model (tables with Oracle/MySQL), Star Schema (fact and dimension tables with Redshift/Teradata), Snowflake Model (normalized dimensions with Snowflake/Redshift), and Network Model (graph structure with Neo4j).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: What does ACID Mean?
docs.text:
Database Transactions

The four key properties of database transactions: Atomicity (all or nothing), Consistency (preserving invariants), Isolation (concurrent transactions are isolated), and Durability (data persistence after commit).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Zero-Copy Optimization
docs.text:
Kafka Internals

A diagram comparing data flow architectures in Kafka. The top section illustrates a standard read process ('Read without zero copy') involving multiple data copies between Application Buffer, OS Buffer, Socket Buffer, and NIC Buffer across User and Kernel contexts. The bottom section illustrates an optimized 'Read with zero copy' process where data is transferred directly from the OS Buffer to the NIC Buffer, minimizing context switches and CPU overhead.

📊 Vergrößern

14 Themen verfügbar

docs.title: 15 System Design Core Concepts
docs.text:
System Design

Titled 'System Design Cheat Sheet' by ByteByteGo that outlines 15 fundamental concepts of system design. It categorizes these concepts into groups such as Requirement Gathering, System Architecture, Data Design, Scalability, Reliability, Availability, Performance, Security, Maintainability, Testing, User Experience Design, Cost Estimation, Documentation, and Migration Plan, providing three key points for each.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: 2024 Edition
docs.text:
System Design

Learn 2024 Edition - The Big Archive. Key concepts and practical implementation.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: 4 Popular Patterns (Client-based, BFF, Monolithic, Federation)
docs.text:
GraphQL

Titled '4 Popular GraphQL Adoption Patterns' illustrating four distinct architectural approaches: Client-based GraphQL (wrapping REST APIs on the client), GraphQL with BFFS (Backend for Frontend layer), The Monolithic GraphQL (single server for multiple clients), and GraphQL Federation (gateway with subgraphs).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: 9 Essential Components of Production Microservice App
docs.text:
System Design

A system architecture diagram illustrating the key components required for a production-ready microservice application. It visualizes the interaction between an API Gateway, Service Registry, Service Layer, Authorization Server, Database Layer with replication, Distributed Cache, Distributed Messaging, Load Balancer, and Observability tools (Metrics via Prometheus/Grafana and Logs via ELK Stack).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Automatic Memory Management Strategies
docs.text:
Garbage Collection 101

Garbage Collection (GC) as an automatic memory management feature. It details and compares specific GC mechanisms and algorithms for Java (Generational GC, G1), Python (Reference Counting, Mark-and-Sweep), and Go (Tricolor Mark-and-Sweep, Hybrid Write Barrier).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: CAP, BASE, SOLID, and KISS Principles
docs.text:
System Design

Four major acronyms in software engineering: CAP (Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance) with a Venn diagram, BASE (Basically Available, Soft State, Eventual Consistency) compared to ACID, SOLID (object-oriented design principles) with illustrative diagrams, and KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) illustrated with Swiss Army knives.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline and DevOps Workflow
docs.text:
How Companies Ship Code To Production

A comprehensive flowchart illustrating the end-to-end software delivery lifecycle. It details five key phases: Plan (creating stories in Jira), Development (coding and committing to GitHub), Build & Package (using Jenkins, JUnit, SonarQube), Test (QA and UAT environments), and Release (deployment to production with monitoring by SREs).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Caching Layers in Distributed Systems
docs.text:
System Design

A system architecture diagram illustrating eight distinct locations where data caching can be implemented. The flow starts from the Client App (1) and CDN (2), moves through a Load Balancer (3) and API Gateway, and branches out to a Message Broker (4), Application Services (5), Distributed Cache like Redis (6), Full-text Search like Elasticsearch (7), and finally the Relational Database (8) which includes internal caching mechanisms like buffer pools and logs.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Class Structure, Components, and Relationships
docs.text:
UML Class Diagram

A comprehensive visual guide detailing the standard notation for UML Class Diagrams. It breaks down the structure of a Class (Name, Attributes, Methods/Operations), defines Interfaces and Enumerations, and illustrates various relationship types including Association, Aggregation, Composition, Inheritance, and Implementation.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Computer Program Execution and System Architecture
docs.text:
How Computer Programs Run

The six stages of how a computer program runs: 1. Program Compilation and Loading, 2. User Interaction and Memory Allocation, 3. System Calls, 4. CPU Status and Memory Structure, 5. Von Neumann Architecture, and 6. Program Termination and Resource Recovery.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Data Management Patterns (Cache Aside, Materialized View, CQRS, Event Sourcing, Index Table, Sharding)
docs.text:
Top 6 Data Management Patterns

Six common data management patterns. Each pattern (Cache Aside, Materialized View, CQRS, Event Sourcing, Index Table, Sharding) is presented with a architectural diagram, its specific purpose, and a list of restrictions or trade-offs.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Data Security and Management Strategies
docs.text:
Cheat Sheet for Managing Sensitive Data

Four key strategies for managing sensitive data: encryption and key management (using HTTPS, salts, and key vaults), data desensitization (using GCM mode), minimal data permissions (via RBAC), and the data lifecycle management process. It also categorizes examples of sensitive data such as PII, health information, and financial records.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Database Scaling and Migration
docs.text:
How Discord Stores Trillions Of Messages

The evolution of Discord's message storage infrastructure. It shows the transition from MongoDB (2015) to Cassandra (2017) due to RAM limitations, and finally to ScyllaDB (2023) to handle trillions of messages and avoid garbage collection pauses. The right side details the modern architecture including a Python API, a Rust Data Service, and the internal components of the Scylla shard-per-core database (Memtable, SSTables, Commit Log).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Deadlock
docs.text:
What is a Deadlock?

The concept of a Deadlock in computer science. It illustrates a scenario where two transactions block each other, lists the four necessary Coffman Conditions (Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, Circular Wait, No Preemption), and outlines strategies for Deadlock Recovery (selecting a victim, rollback) and Deadlock Prevention (resource ordering, timeouts, Banker's Algorithm).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Diagram-as-Code Tools
docs.text:
Top 6 Tools to Turn Code into Beautiful Diagrams

Six software tools that generate diagrams from code, featuring visual examples for Diagrams (Python library), Mermaid (sequence diagrams), Markmap (mind maps), ASCII editors, PlantUML (cloud architecture), and Go-Diagrams.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Differences between Encoding, Encryption, and Tokenization
docs.text:
Encoding vs Encryption vs Tokenization

By ByteByteGo comparing three data protection methods. It is divided into three color-coded sections: Encoding (red), Encryption (orange), and Tokenization (blue). Each section illustrates the process flow (e.g., plain text to cipher text, or PAN to token), the keys or algorithms involved, and specific use cases such as ASCII/Base64 for encoding, HTTPS/Blockchain for encryption, and Credit Card Tokenization/PCI DSS for tokenization.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Distributed System Architecture
docs.text:
System Design Blueprint

A comprehensive architectural diagram illustrating a scalable system design. It details the flow from DNS resolution and load balancing to backend services, database sharding, caching strategies, and specific modules for video uploading and common fan-out services like notifications and analytics.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Endianness (Big Endian vs Little Endian)
docs.text:
Computer Architecture

Learn Endianness (Big Endian vs Little Endian) - Data Representation. Key concepts and practical implementation.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Essential Technical Standards for Software Development
docs.text:
8 Standards Developers Should Know

Eight critical standards for developers: 1. TCP/IP networking layers, 2. HTTP protocol structure, 3. SQL command categories (DDL, DML, etc.), 4. OAuth authorization flow, 5. HTML/CSS specifications and browser compatibility, 6. ECMAScript versions (ES5, ES6, TypeScript), 7. ISO 8601 date/time formatting, and 8. OpenAPI for documentation.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Frontend Performance Tips
docs.text:
Frontend Performance

Titled 'Frontend Performance Cheatsheet' by ByteByteGo. It features a central hub labeled 'Frontend Performance Tips' connected to eight surrounding panels. These panels illustrate specific optimization techniques: Selective Rendering (distinguishing above/below the fold), Code Splitting (breaking large bundles into smaller ones), Compression (zipping files), Loading Sequence (ordering HTML, CSS, JS), Dynamic Imports (loading modules based on user action), Priority-Based Loading (loading critical resources first), Pre-fetching (caching resources proactively), and Tree Shaking (removing unused code from bundles).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: GraphQL Overview and Architecture
docs.text:
What is GraphQL?

From ByteByteGo explaining GraphQL. It is divided into four sections: a history timeline of API technologies (CORBA, SOAP, REST, etc.), a comparison between REST and GraphQL focusing on data fetching and efficiency, a diagram listing the advantages of GraphQL (such as no over-fetching and detailed error tracking), and a technical illustration of how a GraphQL request aggregates data from multiple microservices into a single response.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: High Availability, High Throughput, and High Scalability
docs.text:
System Design

Key system design pillars. It details measurements (such as uptime nines, QPS, TPS, Response Time) and common architectural solutions (like Hot-Hot, Caching, Async Processing, Microservices) for achieving High Availability, High Throughput, and High Scalability.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: How Are Notifications Pushed to Our Devices
docs.text:
Miscellaneous

A technical flowchart illustrating the architecture and workflow of push notifications using Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM). The diagram details a 7-step process: (1-3) Client apps registering with FCM to get a Registration Token, (4) Servers (App Server or Console) building and sending messages, (5) FCM storing messages if devices are offline, (6) FCM forwarding messages to platform transport layers, and (7) Client apps receiving and displaying notifications. It also includes a reference table for FCM credentials like Sender ID, API Key, and App ID.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: How Live Streaming Works
docs.text:
Live Streaming

A flowchart illustrating the end-to-end process of live streaming, divided into three main stages: Video Capture, Server processing (compression, segmentation, streaming server), and View Devices (CDN distribution, decoding, playback). It highlights protocols like RTMP, HLS, and DASH, and notes latency.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Influential Open Source Software
docs.text:
15 Open Source Projects That Changed the World

Categorizing 15 major open-source projects into five key areas: Web Development, Data Management, Developer Tools, Machine Learning & Big Data, and DevOps and Containerization.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Influential Open Source Software Technologies
docs.text:
15 Open Source Projects That Changed the World

15 major open source projects grouped by their primary domain. The categories include Web Development (Node.js, React, Apache HTTP Server), Data Management (PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch), Developer Tools (Git, VSCode, Jupyter Notebook), Machine Learning & Big Data (Tensorflow, Apache Spark, Kafka), and DevOps and Containerization (Docker, Kubernetes, Linux).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Introduction to Cascading Style Sheets
docs.text:
What is CSS?

Six core concepts of CSS: the role of CSS in styling HTML structure, how CSS selectors apply rules, the priority hierarchy of cascading (specificity), layout models (Flexbox and CSS Grids), animations using keyframes, and adaptability (responsive design) across devices.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Key to leverage real-time Data
docs.text:
Change Data Capture

An architectural diagram illustrating the Change Data Capture (CDC) workflow. It shows a 5-step process where a user updates a database, changes are recorded in a transaction log, captured by a Debezium Source Connector, sent to a Message Broker, and finally distributed via Sink Connectors to downstream systems like Redis, a Data Warehouse, and Elasticsearch.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Latency Numbers You Should Know
docs.text:
System Design

A hierarchical pyramid chart illustrating the scale of latency in computer systems, ranging from 1 nanosecond (L1 Cache) to 10 seconds (retry intervals). The chart maps specific hardware and software operations to time scales, with annotations linking real-world technologies like Redis, Memcached, RocksDB, PostgreSQL, Zoom, and Grafana to their corresponding latency tiers.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Linux Text Processing Utilities (grep, cut, sed, awk, sort, uniq)
docs.text:
Top 6

Cheat sheet detailing the top 6 command-line tools for parsing logs. It lists GREP, CUT, SED, AWK, SORT, and UNIQ, providing a basic usage example, a brief description of function, and numbered specific use-cases with command syntax for each tool.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Load Balancing
docs.text:
Load Balancer 101

An educational infographic providing a comprehensive overview of load balancers. It covers the definition and benefits (distributing traffic, scaling, performance, availability), types of load balancers (Hardware, Software, Cloud-based, Layer 4, Layer 7, GSLB), common load balancing algorithms (Round Robin, Sticky, Weighted, Hash, Least Connections, Least Time), and key metrics for monitoring (Traffic, Performance, Health, Error, Load, Security, Availability).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Load Balancing Strategies
docs.text:
Top 6 Load Balancer Use Cases

Six primary use cases for load balancers: Traffic Distribution (splitting traffic by percentage), High Availability (routing around failed services), SSL Termination (decrypting HTTPS traffic before passing to backend), Session Persistence (maintaining user sessions on specific servers), Scalability (distributing load across multiple service instances), and Health Monitoring (checking service status via heartbeats).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Load Balancing Strategies and Patterns
docs.text:
Load Balancer

Six realistic use cases for load balancers: 1. Failure Handling (routing around failed instances), 2. Instance Health Checks (monitoring /health endpoints), 3. Platform Specific Routing (directing traffic based on Desktop vs Mobile), 4. SSL Termination (decrypting HTTPS at the load balancer), 5. Cross Zone Load Balancing (distributing traffic across availability zones), and 6. User Stickiness (maintaining session affinity).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Load Balancing Techniques
docs.text:
Load Balancing Algorithms

Six different load balancing algorithms: Round Robin, Sticky Round Robin, Weighted Round Robin, IP/URL Hash, Least Connections, and Least Time. Each panel demonstrates how a load balancer distributes requests from users (Alice and Bob) to backend services (Service A, B, C) based on the specific logic of that algorithm.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Monolithic vs. Microservices Architecture
docs.text:
Stack Overflow Architecture

Comparing the perceived complex microservices architecture of Stack Overflow (top panel) with its actual simpler monolithic architecture (bottom panel).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Netflix Microservices Stack: API Gateway, BFF, and GraphQL
docs.text:
How NETFLIX Really Uses Java?

Three distinct architectural layers used by Netflix. The top layer shows an API Gateway using Netflix Zuul and Guice. docs.text:
How Git Commands work

The Git workflow, showing the four main areas (Working Directory, Staging Area, Local Repo, Remote Repo) and the specific commands (git add, git commit, git push, git pull, git fetch, git merge, git checkout, git clone) used to move data between them, separated into Local and Remote environments.

docs.title: Git Workflow and Architecture
docs.text:
Git Fundamentals

The flow of data and changes between the four main Git areas: Working Directory, Staging Area, Local Repository, and Remote Repository. It maps specific Git commands (add, commit, push, fetch, pull, merge, checkout, clone) to the movement between these areas.

docs.title: GitOps CI/CD Pipeline Implementation
docs.text:
GitOps Workflow

A complete GitOps workflow. It shows the Continuous Integration phase where code pushed to an App Repo triggers GitHub Actions (build, analysis, tests, security scanning) to create a Docker image. It also shows the Continuous Deployment phase where configuration pushed to a Manifest Repo is synced by Argo CD, which pulls the image from the registry and deploys it to Dev, Test, and Prod Kubernetes environments.

docs.title: Imperative, Object-Oriented, Functional, and Generic Programming
docs.text:
Top 8 programming paradigms

Four major programming paradigms. For each paradigm (Imperative, Object-Oriented, Functional, Generic), it provides a definition, key characteristics (like mutable state, encapsulation, immutability), and examples of programming languages that support them (such as C++, Java, Python, Haskell).

docs.title: JavaScript Fundamentals and Ecosystem
docs.text:
Javascript {.js} Explained

Key JavaScript concepts. It covers language characteristics (interpreted vs compiled, first-class functions, dynamic typing), runtime mechanics (client-side execution, event loop, prototype-based OOP, garbage collection), and the broader ecosystem (comparison with Python/Java, relationship with TypeScript, and popular frameworks like React, Vue, and Angular).

docs.title: Recommended Reading List for Software Engineers
docs.text:
10 Books Every Software Engineer Should Read

Categorizing essential books for software developers into five key areas: General Advice (The Pragmatic Programmer, Code Complete), Coding (Clean Code, Refactoring), Software Architecture (Designing Data-Intensive Applications, System Design Interview), Design Patterns (Design Patterns, Domain-Driven Design), and Data Structures & Algorithms (Introduction to Algorithms, Cracking the Coding Interview).

docs.title: Software Architecture Styles
docs.text:
Software Architecture

Various software architecture styles. It features a central categorization wheel dividing styles into groups like Data-Centric, Layered, Component-Based, and Event-Driven. Surrounding the wheel are detailed diagrams and explanations for specific architectures including CQRS, Layered (n-tier), Microkernel, Microservices, Space-Based, Domain-Driven Design (DDD), Event-Driven, MVP, Interpreter, and Orchestration.

Software Architecture

docs.title: Testing Methodologies and Tools
docs.text:
Software Testing

Table outlining six key software testing processes: Unit Testing, Integration Testing, System Testing, Load Testing, Error Testing, and Test Automation. Each row provides a visual illustration of the testing scope (e.g., individual blocks for unit, connected modules for integration) and lists popular tools associated with that testing type (e.g., pytest, Selenium, JMeter, Jenkins).

docs.title: Timeline of OOP adoption
docs.text:
Object-Oriented Programming

A winding timeline infographic illustrating the history of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) through five numbered stages: Early Concepts (Simula, 1960s-70s), Smalltalk (1970s), C++ Emergence (1980s), OOP Mainstream Adoption (Java and C#, 1990s), and OOP in Modern Times (Python, Javascript, Ruby).

docs.title: Authorization Code Flow
docs.text:
OAuth

The OAuth 2.0 protocol. It defines OAuth as a protocol for sharing user authorization, identifies the three entities involved (User, Server, IdP), compares versions 1.0 and 2.0, lists the four types of OAuth flows, and provides a detailed sequence diagram illustrating the Authorization Code Flow.

docs.text:
Sec_80

Four different OAuth 2.0 authorization flows (Authorization Code, Client Credentials, Implicit Code, and Resource Owner Password), detailing the sequence of requests and responses between the Browser/Client App, Server, and Identity Access Provider (IAP).

Cloud Security

docs.text:
Roadmap for Learning Cyber Security

A detailed mind map illustrating the key domains of cybersecurity. The central node 'Cybersecurity Domains' branches out into nine major categories: Security Architecture, Frameworks and Standard, Security Operation, Physical Security, User Education, Career Development, Threat Intelligence, Governance, and Risk Assessment. Each category further breaks down into specific sub-topics like Cryptography, NIST, SIEM, Audit, and Penetration Testing.

docs.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) Types, Mitigation, and Attack Flow
docs.text:
XSS

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities. It details the mechanisms of Reflected and Stored XSS attacks through diagrams, lists four key mitigation strategies (Implement CSP, Input Sanitization, Escaping Output, HTTP Only Cookies), and depicts a specific attack scenario where an attacker uses a redirect to inject a script and steal user cookies.

docs.title: Cybersecurity Ecosystem, Threats, and Frameworks
docs.text:
Cybersecurity 101

Titled 'Cybersecurity 101' by ByteByteGo. It visualizes the core components of cybersecurity including the top 15 threats, definitions, importance statistics, the CIA Triad (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability), major frameworks like NIST and Microsoft Zero-Trust, basic defense mechanisms (layered security), and a detailed map of the cybersecurity ecosystem featuring hardware, people, software, network, and compliance vendors.

docs.title: DevSecOps Principles and Practices
docs.text:
What is DevSecOps?

The core components of DevSecOps. It features a central wheel integrating DEV, SEC, and OPS, surrounded by detailed segments explaining key practices such as Security Checks & Scans, Continuous Monitoring, CI/CD, Infrastructure as Code (IaC), Container Security, Key Management, Threat Modeling, QA Integration, Collaboration & Communication, and Vulnerability Management.

docs.title: JWT Structure, Authentication Workflow, and Signing Algorithms
docs.text:
JWT (JSON Web Token)

An educational infographic divided into four panels explaining JSON Web Tokens. Panel 1 defines JSON format. Panel 2 details the 3-part JWT structure (Header, Data, Signature) and Base64 encoding. Panel 3 illustrates the authentication workflow (Login, Validation, Token creation). Panel 4 explains signing algorithms, comparing Public Key (Asymmetric) and Symmetric Key methods.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Salted Password Hashing
docs.text:
How to store passwords in DB?

The image illustrates the secure process for handling user passwords in a database. It is divided into two workflows: the top section shows how to 'Store a password' by combining a user-provided password with a randomly generated salt, hashing the result, and saving both the salt and hash to the DB. The bottom section shows how to 'Validate a password' by retrieving the stored salt, hashing the input password with it, and comparing the new hash against the stored hash to check for equality.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Data Pipeline Architecture and Stages
docs.text:
Data Pipeline

A vertical flowchart diagram illustrating the end-to-end lifecycle of a data pipeline. It categorizes the process into five main stages indicated by arrows on the right: Collect (Data Stores, Streams, Applications), Ingest (Data Load, Event Queue), Store (Data Lake, Data Warehouse, Data Lakehouse), Compute (Batch Processing, Stream Processing), and Consume (Data Science, Business Intelligence, Self-Service Analytics, ML Services).

docs.title: Lookaside Cache, Transient Data Store, Primary Store, High Volume Lookups
docs.text:
Caching

Four specific caching architectures used by Netflix: Lookaside Cache (checking cache then backend), Transient Data Store (writing directly to cache), Primary Store (using cache as the main database with offline precomputation), and High Volume Lookups (precomputing static data like translations).

Learn New York Times, CDC, Microservice Connector - Use Cases. Key concepts and practical implementation.

docs.title: Notification Delivery Workflow
docs.text:
Design a Notification Push System

A system architecture diagram illustrating the end-to-end flow of a notification push system. It depicts business services (Order, Payment) sending notifications to a Notification Gateway. The flow continues through a Notification Distribution module (handling validation, scheduling, priority, and templates), interacts with user channel preferences and a template repository, and moves to a Notification Router using queues. Finally, notifications are sent via various channels (Email, In-App, SMS, Social Media), with parallel processes for tracing, analytics, and operations monitoring.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: QR Code Payment Transaction Flow
docs.text:
Payment Systems

The 12-step process of a 'scan to pay' transaction, showing the interactions between a Consumer (Digital Wallet App), a Merchant (Checkout Counter), and a Payment Service Provider (PSP Gateway).

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Scan to Pay Transaction Workflow
docs.text:
Payment Systems

A technical diagram illustrating the 12-step process of a QR code-based payment. It details the interactions between three main entities: the Consumer (using a Digital Wallet App), the Merchant (at a Checkout Counter), and the Payment Service Provider (PSP). The flow covers generating a dynamic QR code, scanning it with a mobile app, authorizing the payment, and confirming the transaction success to both parties.

docs.title: Should we send a notification?
docs.text:
Notification System

A detailed flowchart illustrating the decision-making algorithm for sending user notifications. It evaluates multiple conditions including channel mute status, Do Not Disturb (DnD) mode, specific mention types (@channel, @here), thread subscriptions, and granular user preferences for both channel and global notification settings.

docs.title: Top 8 Cache Eviction Strategies
docs.text:
Caching

Eight common cache eviction algorithms and strategies: Least Recently Used (LRU), Most Recently Used (MRU), Segmented LRU (SLRU), Least Frequently Used (LFU), First In First Out (FIFO), Time-to-Live (TTL), Two-Tiered Caching, and Random Replacement (RR). It also includes a famous quote by Phil Karlton about cache invalidation.

📊 Vergrößern

docs.title: Visa Revenue Model
docs.text:
Payment Systems

A flowchart diagram illustrating how Visa makes money through transaction fees. It depicts the flow of funds between a Cardholder, Merchant, Issuing Bank, Acquiring Bank, and the Card Network. The diagram breaks down the 'merchant discount fee' ($2.00) into the 'interchange fee' ($1.75) paid to the issuer and the 'acquiring markup' ($0.25) kept by the acquirer, while also noting network assessments and interest fees.
